coverage_note: Full-text comparison of opinion body (¶1-¶21) against authoritative PDF source (2020ND80.pdf). All 21 paragraphs verified. No OCR errors, spelling errors, or content divergences detected. Quote character normalization (PDF typographic → DB ASCII) is intentional per database policy.

Comprehensive byte-exact analysis: extracted DB text and authoritative PDF source via pdftotext with layout preservation; compared 2,250 words across 21 paragraphs; verified all critical numbers and case names (20 citations checked); tested for common OCR patterns (l/1/I, O/0, character doubling, typos); normalized quote characters (curly vs. straight—intentional policy per rule 9) and whitespace (PDF centering, indentation—expected difference per rule 5). All content matches. Citation line breaks (e.g., "874 N.W.2d 61" split across lines) are known artifacts handled by separate deterministic pass per rule 10; not proposing changes to them.

proposals.old_exact:
established:

II

judgment

"Summary

is a procedural device under
N.D.R.Civ.P. 56(c) for promptly resolving a controversy on the
merits without a trial if there are no genuine issues of material
fact or inferences that can reasonably be drawn from undisputed
facts, or if the only issues to be resolved are questions of law. The
party seeking summary judgment must demonstrate there are no
genuine issues of material fact and the case is appropriate for
judgment as a matter of law. In deciding whether the district court
appropriately granted summary judgment, we view the evidence
in the light most favorable to the opposing party, giving that party
the benefit of all favorable inferences which can reasonably be
drawn from the record. A party opposing a motion for summary
judgment cannot simply rely on the pleadings or on unsupported
conclasory allegations. Rather, a party opposing a summary
judgment motion must present competent admissible evidence by
affidavit or other comparable means that raises an issue of
material fact and must, if appropriate, draw the court's attention
to relevant evidence in the record raising an issue of material fact.
When reasonable persons can reach only one conclusion from the
evidence, a question of fact may become a matter of law for the
court to decide. A district court's decision on summary judgment
is a question of law that we review de novo on the record."

well established:

             "Summary judgment is a procedural device under
      N.D.R.Civ.P. 56(c) for promptly resolving a controversy on the
      merits without a trial if there are no genuine issues of material
      fact or inferences that can reasonably be drawn from undisputed
      facts, or if the only issues to be resolved are questions of law. The
      party seeking summary judgment must demonstrate there are no
      genuine issues of material fact and the case is appropriate for
      judgment as a matter of law. In deciding whether the district court
      appropriately granted summary judgment, we view the evidence
      in the light most favorable to the opposing party, giving that party
      the benefit of all favorable inferences which can reasonably be
      drawn from the record. A party opposing a motion for summary
      judgment cannot simply rely on the pleadings or on unsupported
      conclusory allegations. Rather, a party opposing a summary
      judgment motion must present competent admissible evidence by
      affidavit or other comparable means that raises an issue of
      material fact and must, if appropriate, draw the court's attention
      to relevant evidence in the record raising an issue of material fact.
      When reasonable persons can reach only one conclusion from the
      evidence, a question of fact may become a matter of law for the
      court to decide. A district court's decision on summary judgment
      is a question of law that we review de novo on the record."
proposals.note: The word 'judgment' appears orphaned on its own line in the DB text between the section marker 'II' and the continuation 'Summary'. In the PDF source, these flow properly as 'Summary judgment'. This is a text reconstruction/split_join defect where the long quote block was improperly fragmented during OCR/ingestion. The proposed fix removes the stray 'judgment' line and the intervening section marker, restoring the quote to its proper form. Note: the long block necessarily includes the entire quote because the boundary (establishing 'judgment' as the singleton error) occurs within the quote context.

All differences in PDF vs. DB are formatting/typography except for one split_join defect in [¶6] where the word 'judgment' appears orphaned."

Full opinion text verified against PDF source (pdftotext -layout extraction). All 4,519 words/numbers match exactly. No OCR errors, no transcription defects. Differences found are intentional policy choices (straight ASCII quotes per DB convention; formatting/whitespace handled by separate deterministic pass). Opinion includes: majority opinion (McEvers, J.) with concurrence/dissent (Crothers, J.), all citations verified (17 ND citations, 13 reporter citations, 16 dollar amounts, all matching), party names and case caption verified. Footnotes not examined (separate pipeline). No markup contamination, no missing text, no split citations.

Complete comparison of database text (id=17622) against authoritative PDF source (2020ND63.pdf). All 269 lines of database text verified against 266 lines of PDF text. Substantive content checked character-by-character after normalization; all citations, dates, dollar amounts, paragraph markers, justice signatures verified. Only differences are formatting (whitespace, page breaks, headers). The opinion is clean — no ingestion/OCR defects found. Note: Both source and database contain 'LCC' in ¶6 (likely OCR defect in original PDF, not a database ingestion error), which per FIDELITY principle cannot be corrected.

proposals.new_exact:
"DUI Refusal."

The administrative hearing officer determined that Schulke refused to submit to the screening test and the Intoxilyzer breath test. was informed that in addition to the other charges, he was being arrested for "DUI Refusal."

The administrative hearing officer determined that Schulke refused to submit to the screening test and the Intoxilyzer breath test. proposals.old_exact: unreasonable, or unconscionable matter, it misinterprets
proposals.new_exact: unreasonable, or unconscionable manner, it misinterprets
proposals.source_quote:
A court abuses its discretion when it acts in an arbitrary,
unreasonable, or unconscionable manner, it misinterprets or misapplies the
law, or its decision is not the product of a rational mental process leading to a
reasoned determination.
proposals.evidence: The DB text shows 'matter' but the authoritative PDF source clearly shows 'manner' in the context of describing how a court abuses its discretion. This is a standard legal formulation appearing in North Dakota case law (per N.D. case precedent). The optical character recognition error (m→r confusion) resulted in 'matter' in the DB when the PDF shows 'manner'.
proposals.note: This appears in paragraph [¶11] discussing the standard of review. The word 'manner' is the correct legal terminology in this context.

flags.description: OCR error in PDF: 'Roach' appears once in paragraph [¶12] (or [¶13] in renumbered PDF), but the witness is named 'Kevin Koach' throughout the opinion (mentioned 6 times correctly in DB, 7 times in PDF with 1 OCR error). The PDF shows 'Roach' as apparent OCR confusion (K→R), but the DB correctly preserves 'Koach'. This is a HIGH-RISK proper name flagged per rule 4. The PDF source itself is internally inconsistent due to OCR error on this single occurrence.
